title: FastAPI Reference Template
subtitle: A reference guide for building FastAPI services with emphasis on consistency and simplicity
description: Covers Swagger metadata, architecture, async database layer, ORM models, Pydantic schemas, CRUD routers, sub-collection endpoints, and MCP server integration.

lessons.title: Swagger Overview
lessons.guide:
## Swagger Overview

The left panel is the **live Swagger UI** — auto-generated from your Python code. Everything you see here is driven by metadata you set in two places: the `FastAPI()` constructor and the router decorators.

### App-level metadata (`main.py`)

The `FastAPI()` call configures the top of the Swagger page:

```python
app = FastAPI(
    title="FastAPI Reference Template",
    description=(
        "Our template to be used as a reference to guide "
        "how we build our FastAPI services, with an emphasis "
        "on consistency and simplicity."
    ),
    version="1.0.0",
    contact={"name": "API Team", "email": "api-team@example.com"},
    openapi_tags=openapi_tags,
)
```

| Parameter | Where it shows up |
|-----------|-------------------|
| `title` | Large heading at the top |
| `description` | Below the title (supports **markdown**) |
| `version` | Badge next to the title |
| `contact` | Expandable info section |
| `openapi_tags` | Sidebar section headings |

### Tag definitions control the sidebar

The collapsible sections ("Users", "Groups") come from `openapi_tags`:

```python
openapi_tags = [
    {
        "name": "Users",
        "description": "Operations on the **User** resource, "
                       "including sub-collection membership.",
    },
    {
        "name": "Groups",
        "description": "Operations on the **Group** resource.",
    },
]
```

Each tag becomes a section. The `description` supports markdown — use it to explain what the resource is. The order in this list controls the order in the sidebar.

### Endpoint-level metadata (routers)

Every `@router` decorator must include five fields:

```python
@router.get(
    "",
    tags=["Users"],
    summary="List users",
    description="Retrieve a paginated list of all users.",
    response_model=UserListResponse,
    status_code=status.HTTP_200_OK,
)
```

| Field | What it does | If missing |
|-------|-------------|------------|
| `tags` | Places endpoint under a sidebar section | Appears under "default" |
| `summary` | One-liner on the collapsed row | Shows raw path instead |
| `description` | Detailed text when expanded | No explanation |
| `response_model` | Generates response schema | No response docs |
| `status_code` | Documented success code | Defaults to 200 |

> **Rule:** All five are mandatory on every endpoint. Missing any degrades the Swagger output.

### The Schemas section

Scroll to the bottom of Swagger — every Pydantic model is listed with field-level descriptions. This comes from using `Field()`:

```python
display_name: str = Field(
    ...,
    description="The user's full display name.",
    examples=["Ada Lovelace"],
)
```

The `examples` values pre-fill the "Try it out" request bodies.

> **Tip:** Download the **Swagger Metadata Reference** from the sidebar for a printable checklist version of these rules.

diagrams.title: Entity Relationship Diagram

lessons.guide:
## Architecture Overview

The left panel shows two diagrams that map out the application.

### Request Flow

Shows how an HTTP request travels through the app:

1. **Client** sends a request (e.g. `GET /v1/users`)
2. **main.py** routes it to the correct router
3. **Router** validates input via Pydantic schemas, then queries via SQLAlchemy models
4. **Model** talks to the database and returns results
5. **Schema** validates the response before sending it back

### Entity Relationship Diagram

The database has three tables:

- **USERS** — `id`, `display_name`, `email` (unique)
- **GROUPS** — `id`, `display_name`, `description`
- **USER_GROUP_ASSOCIATION** — join table with `user_id` + `group_id` (composite PK)

This is a classic many-to-many: one user can belong to many groups, one group can contain many users. The association table has no extra columns — it's purely a link.

> **Key takeaway:** The architecture is intentionally flat. No deep nesting, no abstract base classes, no service layers. Each file has one job and a predictable name.

lessons.title: Database & Sessions
lessons.guide:
## Async Database Layer

### Database-first approach

We design the schema directly in PostgreSQL first — tables, constraints, relationships, indexes — then build the SQLAlchemy models to match. The database is the source of truth for structure; the code mirrors it.

For this reference template we use SQLite for portability, but in production you'd point `DATABASE_URL` at your PostgreSQL instance.

### Why async?

FastAPI runs on an async event loop. Synchronous DB calls block the entire server. Async SQLAlchemy lets other requests continue while a query is in flight.

### The engine

```python
engine = create_async_engine(DATABASE_URL, echo=False)
```

- `create_async_engine` — async version of SQLAlchemy's engine
- `echo=False` — set to `True` during debugging to see generated SQL
- The URL comes from `config.py` — one string to change per environment

### Session factory

```python
async_session = async_sessionmaker(
    engine,
    class_=AsyncSession,
    expire_on_commit=False,
)
```

`expire_on_commit=False` is important — without it, accessing model attributes after `commit()` triggers lazy loads, which don't work in async context.

### The `get_db` dependency

```python
async def get_db():
    async with async_session() as session:
        yield session
```

This is a **FastAPI dependency**. Endpoints declare it as a parameter:

```python
async def list_users(db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)):
```

The session lifecycle is fully automatic:
1. **Created** when the request arrives
2. **Injected** into the endpoint function
3. **Closed** when the request finishes

> You never manually open or close sessions in endpoints. The dependency handles it.

lessons.title: ORM Models & Relationships
lessons.guide:
## SQLAlchemy Models

These models mirror the tables you've already created in the database. They don't create the schema — they describe it so SQLAlchemy knows how to query it.

### The User model

```python
class UserModel(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users"

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    display_name = Column(String(255), nullable=False)
    email = Column(String(255), nullable=False, unique=True)

    groups = relationship(
        "GroupModel",
        secondary=user_group_association,
        back_populates="users",
        lazy="selectin",
    )
```

### Many-to-many via association table

Users and Groups are connected through a join table:

```python
user_group_association = Table(
    "user_group_association",
    Base.metadata,
    Column("user_id", Integer,
           ForeignKey("users.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
           primary_key=True),
    Column("group_id", Integer,
           ForeignKey("groups.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
           primary_key=True),
)
```

Both models reference this via `secondary=user_group_association`.

### Why `lazy="selectin"`?

| Strategy | Behaviour | Tradeoff |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| `lazy` (default) | Loads on attribute access | N+1 queries |
| `joined` | Always JOINs | Heavy when you don't need relations |
| `selectin` | Batch SELECT with IN clause | Best balance for async |

`selectin` fires one extra query to load all related objects. Works well with async and avoids N+1.

### `ondelete="CASCADE"`

When a user or group is deleted, the DB automatically removes association rows. No orphaned references.

> **Convention:** One model per file, named `{resource}_model.py`.

lessons.title: Pydantic Schemas
lessons.guide:
## Schema Design Pattern

Every resource follows a **four-schema** pattern:

### 1. Base — shared required fields

```python
class UserBase(BaseModel):
    display_name: str = Field(
        ...,
        description="The user's full display name.",
        examples=["Ada Lovelace"],
    )
    email: str = Field(
        ...,
        description="The user's unique email address.",
        examples=["ada@example.com"],
    )
```

### 2. Create — inherits Base

```python
class UserCreate(UserBase):
    pass  # All Base fields are required
```

### 3. Update — all fields optional

```python
class UserUpdate(BaseModel):
    display_name: str | None = Field(default=None, ...)
    email: str | None = Field(default=None, ...)
```

Update does **not** inherit Base — every field is independently optional for true PATCH semantics.

### 4. Response — Base + server fields

```python
class UserResponse(UserBase):
    id: int = Field(..., description="Server-generated unique ID.")
    model_config = {"from_attributes": True}
```

`from_attributes = True` lets Pydantic read from SQLAlchemy model instances directly.

### Why `Field()` on everything?

`description` and `examples` flow into the OpenAPI spec:
- Swagger shows descriptions next to every field
- "Try it out" pre-fills example values
- The Schemas section becomes real documentation

> **Rule:** Never use bare `name: str`. Always use `Field(description=..., examples=[...])`.

lessons.title: User CRUD Router
lessons.guide:
## Resource-Oriented Endpoints (AIP Style)

### The five standard methods

| Method | Path | Status | Purpose |
|--------|------|--------|--------|
| `GET` | `/v1/users` | 200 | List (paginated) |
| `GET` | `/v1/users/{id}` | 200 | Get single resource |
| `POST` | `/v1/users` | 201 | Create new resource |
| `PATCH` | `/v1/users/{id}` | 200 | Partial update |
| `DELETE` | `/v1/users/{id}` | 204 | Delete (no body) |

### Pagination — the "fetch one extra" trick

```python
result = await db.execute(
    select(UserModel).offset(offset).limit(limit + 1)
)
rows = result.scalars().all()
has_next = len(rows) > limit
users = rows[:limit]
```

Request `limit + 1` rows. If you get them all, there's a next page. Return `next_offset` so clients know where to continue. No separate COUNT query needed.

### Partial update (PATCH)

```python
for field, value in payload.model_dump(exclude_unset=True).items():
    setattr(user, field, value)
```

`exclude_unset=True` only includes fields the client actually sent. Send `{"email": "new@example.com"}` and only email changes.

### Endpoint decorator checklist

```python
@router.post(
    "",
    tags=["Users"],
    summary="Create a user",
    description="Create a new user resource.",
    response_model=UserResponse,
    status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED,
)
```

> Without `response_model`, Swagger can't show the response schema. Without `status_code`, everything defaults to 200. Without `summary`, endpoints show as raw paths.

lessons.title: Sub-Collection Endpoints
lessons.guide:
## Many-to-Many as Sub-Resources

### The AIP pattern

Instead of a flat `/v1/memberships` endpoint, model the relationship as a sub-resource of each parent. For a many-to-many, both sides get sub-collection endpoints:

```text
GET  /v1/users/{user_id}/groups    ← list user's groups
POST /v1/users/{user_id}/groups    ← assign user to group
GET  /v1/groups/{group_id}/users   ← list group's users
```

Reads naturally: "get the groups **of** this user" or "get the users **in** this group".

### List a user's groups (`user_router.py`)

```python
@router.get("/{user_id}/groups")
async def list_user_groups(user_id: int, db=Depends(get_db)):
    user = await db.get(UserModel, user_id)
    if not user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="User not found.")
    return [GroupResponse.model_validate(g) for g in user.groups]
```

### List a group's users (`group_router.py`)

```python
@router.get("/{group_id}/users")
async def list_group_users(group_id: int, db=Depends(get_db)):
    group = await db.get(GroupModel, group_id)
    if not group:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Group not found.")
    return [UserResponse.model_validate(u) for u in group.users]
```

Both work because `lazy="selectin"` on the relationship means related objects are already loaded.

### Assign a user to a group (`user_router.py`)

```python
@router.post("/{user_id}/groups")
async def assign_user_to_group(
    user_id: int,
    payload: AssignGroupRequest,
    db=Depends(get_db),
):
    user = await db.get(UserModel, user_id)
    group = await db.get(GroupModel, payload.group_id)
    if group in user.groups:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=409, detail="Already a member.")
    user.groups.append(group)
    await db.commit()
```

Request body is just `{"group_id": 1}`. The 409 prevents duplicates.

### Where each endpoint lives

| Endpoint | Router file |
|----------|-------------|
| `GET /v1/users/{id}/groups` | `user_router.py` |
| `POST /v1/users/{id}/groups` | `user_router.py` |
| `GET /v1/groups/{id}/users` | `group_router.py` |

The sub-collection lives in the **parent's** router. Users own the "assign" action because the request is "add this user to a group".

### Error codes

| Status | When |
|--------|------|
| 404 | User or group doesn't exist |
| 409 | Duplicate assignment |

> Always validate the parent resource exists before touching sub-resources.

lessons.title: MCP Server Integration
lessons.guide:
## Exposing Your API to AI Agents via MCP

The `mcp_server/` directory contains an MCP server that automatically exposes FastAPI endpoints as tools that AI agents can call.

### How it works

1. The MCP server starts and fetches `/openapi.json` from the running FastAPI app
2. It reads `mcp_config.yaml` to determine which endpoints to expose
3. It registers matching endpoints as MCP tools
4. AI agents (Claude, Kiro, etc.) can then call tools like `list_users`, `create_user`, `get_user`

Because your Swagger metadata is thorough (`summary`, `description`, `response_model`), the MCP tools inherit rich descriptions automatically. Good API docs = good AI tool descriptions.

### Controlling which endpoints are exposed

Edit `mcp_server/mcp_config.yaml` to control access:

```yaml
# Expose everything except destructive operations
mode: exclude
endpoints:
  - "DELETE *"
  - "PATCH /v1/users/{user_id}"
```

```yaml
# Only expose read-only endpoints
mode: include
endpoints:
  - "GET *"
```

| Mode | Behaviour |
|------|----------|
| `include` | Only listed patterns become tools (allowlist) |
| `exclude` | Everything becomes a tool except listed patterns (blocklist) |

Patterns support wildcards: `*` matches any method or path segment.

### Tool naming convention

| Endpoint | Tool name |
|----------|----------|
| `GET /v1/users` | `list_users` |
| `GET /v1/users/{id}` | `get_user` |
| `POST /v1/users` | `create_user` |
| `PATCH /v1/users/{id}` | `update_user` |
| `DELETE /v1/users/{id}` | `delete_user` |
| `GET /v1/users/{id}/groups` | `list_user_groups` |
| `POST /v1/users/{id}/groups` | `assign_user_group` |

### Running it

```bash
# Terminal 1: start the API
uvicorn app.main:app --reload

# Terminal 2: start the MCP server
python -m mcp_server
```

On startup it logs how many tools were registered vs skipped.

### Configuring in your IDE

Copy `mcp_server/mcp.json.example` to your IDE's MCP config:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "fastapi-reference": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["-m", "mcp_server"],
      "env": { "PYTHONPATH": "." }
    }
  }
}
```

> **Key point:** The quality of your Swagger metadata directly determines how well AI agents understand and use your API. This is another reason every endpoint needs `summary`, `description`, and `response_model`.

lessons.title: Try the API Live
lessons.guide:
## Hands-On Exercises

### Exercise 1: Create and retrieve

1. Expand **POST /v1/users** → click "Try it out"
2. Send: `{"display_name": "Ada Lovelace", "email": "ada@example.com"}`
3. Note the `id` in the response
4. Use **GET /v1/users/{user_id}** with that ID

### Exercise 2: Partial update

1. Use **PATCH /v1/users/{user_id}**
2. Send only: `{"display_name": "Ada L."}`
3. GET the user again — email should be unchanged

### Exercise 3: Many-to-many

1. Create a group: **POST /v1/groups** with `{"display_name": "Engineering"}`
2. Assign: **POST /v1/users/1/groups** with `{"group_id": 1}`
3. List: **GET /v1/users/1/groups**
4. Assign again — expect a **409 Conflict**

### Exercise 4: Pagination

1. Create 3+ users
2. **GET /v1/users** with `limit=2`
3. Check `next_offset` in the response
4. Call again with `offset=2`

### What to look for

- Every request body field has a description and example
- Response schemas show exactly what comes back
- Scroll to the **Schemas** section at the bottom
- Error responses use proper HTTP status codes
